title: Final Assessment: Test-Driven Development in Python

questions.question: Which statement best matches the course's view of canonical TDD?

options.A: It is mainly a requirement to write tests before implementation exists
options.B: It is a behavior-design loop that uses small failing tests and refactoring to shape code incrementally
options.C: It is mostly a coverage-management technique
options.D: It is only useful when every dependency is mocked

questions.question: What is the strongest default posture for reliable Python test isolation?

options.A: Broad shared fixtures first, then narrow them later
options.B: Strong default isolation with explicit cleanup and narrowly justified sharing
options.C: One event loop and one database reused across the full suite
options.D: Mock all stateful code to avoid lifecycle design

questions.question: Why does the course recommend pytest as the modern default without rejecting unittest entirely?

options.A: Because pytest is newer and therefore always superior
options.B: Because pytest reduces friction in practice, while unittest fluency still matters for inherited codebases and migration paths
options.C: Because unittest cannot run on Python 3
options.D: Because pytest removes the need to think about framework tradeoffs

questions.question: What is the best reason to hide an external API behind an owned seam?

options.A: To avoid ever testing the boundary again
options.B: To keep business logic expressed in application-owned concepts instead of foreign transport detail
options.C: To increase file count for maintainability metrics
options.D: To guarantee the vendor can never change

questions.question: Which testing choice best reflects the course's treatment of external boundaries?

options.A: Deep mocks are always preferable because they are fastest
options.B: Strict mocks belong mainly at owned collaborators, while external boundaries often need verified fakes, contract checks, or integration tests
options.C: Only end-to-end tests matter for third-party systems
options.D: Contract testing and deep mocking are interchangeable terms

questions.question: What principle organizes advanced coverage in a non-trivial Python system?

options.A: Use every available testing tool once the system gets larger
options.B: Choose test layers by the distinct truth each one needs to provide
options.D: Put all verification inside the database layer

questions.question: Explain how the course would tell a team to combine disciplined TDD practice with honest evidence calibration.

required_points: Use behavior-first, isolation-aware, boundary-conscious testing practices
Avoid universal claims about guaranteed outcomes
Measure effects locally and keep open limits explicit
answer_key.full_credit: Connects the technical practice model with the course's context-sensitive evidence stance
answer_key.partial_credit: Mentions practice or evidence caution but does not integrate them

questions.prompt: Design a TDD strategy for a Python service that includes domain logic, async execution, database writes, and an external API. Your answer should explain the red-green-refactor loop, isolation model, framework choices, seam design, mock/fake/contract judgment, broader specification techniques, and how you would communicate expected outcomes honestly to stakeholders.
questions.guidance: Strong answers integrate the course end to end: disciplined design loops, explicit lifecycle control, owned boundaries, truth-preserving test choice, and evidence-calibrated adoption language.

rubric.A: Integrates the course's major ideas into one coherent, credible workflow and treats outcome claims with appropriate discipline
rubric.B: Covers several major ideas correctly but leaves one important boundary or evidence issue underdeveloped
rubric.C: Describes TDD generically without applying the course's framework for boundaries, realism, and evidence
